texts.label: text
texts.orig: Abstract -  Th.e  principle and  engineering aspects of  the  membrane gas  absorption process are discussed. It  is  shown that  membrane gas  absorbers have  size  advantages over  conventional equipment. A  suitable choice of  absorption liquids and  membranes is  necessary to  achieve a workable system.  Significant reductions in  the  energy  consumption of absorption processes can be achieved. For applications in the horticultural industry the membrane gas absorption process leads  to  a  cost-effective way  of carbon dioxide production.
texts.text: Abstract -  Th.e  principle and  engineering aspects of  the  membrane gas  absorption process are discussed. It  is  shown that  membrane gas  absorbers have  size  advantages over  conventional equipment. A  suitable choice of  absorption liquids and  membranes is  necessary to  achieve a workable system.  Significant reductions in  the  energy  consumption of absorption processes can be achieved. For applications in the horticultural industry the membrane gas absorption process leads  to  a  cost-effective way  of carbon dioxide production.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In  the  Netherlands carbon dioxide enrichment in  greenhouses is  widely practised. Increasing the  carbon dioxide concentration in  the  greenhouse from  350  ppm  to  500  ppm  results in  a production increase of  25% for  certain bulk crops. Carbon dioxide is  currently generated by  the  combustion of  natural gas  which is wasteful as  the resulting energy produced is  not always utilised. The resulting flue gas  may  also  contain impurities such as  nitrous oxides and ethene which are  detrimental to  plant growth. Furthermore carbon dioxide requirement is  high during summer when there is  little or  no energy requirement. The current process is  therefore not  an  energy-efficient process. Carbon dioxide can  also  be  produced in  a cogeneration plant with an absorption process. The waste heat  from the cogeneration plant is  used to  regenerate the absorption liquid. The carbon dioxide thus produced is  of  high purity and can be  used for  enrichment without detrimental consequences for  the  plants.
texts.text: In  the  Netherlands carbon dioxide enrichment in  greenhouses is  widely practised. Increasing the  carbon dioxide concentration in  the  greenhouse from  350  ppm  to  500  ppm  results in  a production increase of  25% for  certain bulk crops. Carbon dioxide is  currently generated by  the  combustion of  natural gas  which is wasteful as  the resulting energy produced is  not always utilised. The resulting flue gas  may  also  contain impurities such as  nitrous oxides and ethene which are  detrimental to  plant growth. Furthermore carbon dioxide requirement is  high during summer when there is  little or  no energy requirement. The current process is  therefore not  an  energy-efficient process. Carbon dioxide can  also  be  produced in  a cogeneration plant with an absorption process. The waste heat  from the cogeneration plant is  used to  regenerate the absorption liquid. The carbon dioxide thus produced is  of  high purity and can be  used for  enrichment without detrimental consequences for  the  plants.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Carbon dioxide can be selectively removed from flue gases using commercially available absorption processes and conventional equipment. The processes are, however, energy-intensive and the resulting equipment is  rather large.  There is  a  need for  smaller size  absorbers especially in retro-fit situations and situations where space and weight is  limited as  is  for  instance the case  on oilplatforms. Membrane gas absorption is  a  process which leads to  smaller absorbers through the use  of  small diameter hollow fibre membranes. The  principle is  shown in  Figure 1.
texts.text: Carbon dioxide can be selectively removed from flue gases using commercially available absorption processes and conventional equipment. The processes are, however, energy-intensive and the resulting equipment is  rather large.  There is  a  need for  smaller size  absorbers especially in retro-fit situations and situations where space and weight is  limited as  is  for  instance the case  on oilplatforms. Membrane gas absorption is  a  process which leads to  smaller absorbers through the use  of  small diameter hollow fibre membranes. The  principle is  shown in  Figure 1.

texts.orig: FERON and JANSEN:  CAPTURE  OF CO?  USING  MEMBRANE  GAS  ABSORPTION

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The essential element in  a  membrane absorber is  a  porous polymer membrane. The gas  phase  remains separated from  the  liquid absorbent as a result of the  hydrophobicity of the  membrane. A flue  gas stream  is fed  along  one  side of  the  membrane. The components to  be  removed from  the  gas  stream will  diffuse  through the  gas  filled  pores of the  membrane. On  the  other side  of the  membrane they  will  be  absorbed into the  absorption liquid. The  use  of a membrane absorber leads  to  the  following advantages:
texts.text: The essential element in  a  membrane absorber is  a  porous polymer membrane. The gas  phase  remains separated from  the  liquid absorbent as a result of the  hydrophobicity of the  membrane. A flue  gas stream  is fed  along  one  side of  the  membrane. The components to  be  removed from  the  gas  stream will  diffuse  through the  gas  filled  pores of the  membrane. On  the  other side  of the  membrane they  will  be  absorbed into the  absorption liquid. The  use  of a membrane absorber leads  to  the  following advantages:

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: -gas  and liquid flow  are  independent resulting in  avoidance of  problems encountered in  packed/tray columns such  as flooding, foaming, channeling and  entrainment,
texts.text: -gas  and liquid flow  are  independent resulting in  avoidance of  problems encountered in  packed/tray columns such  as flooding, foaming, channeling and  entrainment,

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The choice of  a  suitable combination of  absorption liquid and  membrane is  very  important. The hydrophobicity and pore characteristics of  the  membrane material should be  such that the  pores remain gas-filled at  an  excess pressure of  at  least 0.5  bar and preferably more than 1  bar on the liquid side. Commercially available porous polyolefin membranes appear not to be suitable for operation with the usual absorption liquid, i.e. aqueous solutions of  monoethanolamine. In  the  course of time  the  liquid will  seep  through these  membranes as  a result of the  low  surface tension of  the  organic liquid/water mixtures. As  a  membrane material teflon seems  a better choice because it  is  more hydrophobic and more inert towards aqueous monoethanolamine solutions. The required maximal pore  size  can  be  determined from  the  Laplace-Young equation:
texts.text: The choice of  a  suitable combination of  absorption liquid and  membrane is  very  important. The hydrophobicity and pore characteristics of  the  membrane material should be  such that the  pores remain gas-filled at  an  excess pressure of  at  least 0.5  bar and preferably more than 1  bar on the liquid side. Commercially available porous polyolefin membranes appear not to be suitable for operation with the usual absorption liquid, i.e. aqueous solutions of  monoethanolamine. In  the  course of time  the  liquid will  seep  through these  membranes as  a result of the  low  surface tension of  the  organic liquid/water mixtures. As  a  membrane material teflon seems  a better choice because it  is  more hydrophobic and more inert towards aqueous monoethanolamine solutions. The required maximal pore  size  can  be  determined from  the  Laplace-Young equation:

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Pressure difference between liquid and  gas
texts.text: Pressure difference between liquid and  gas

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Contact angle  for  liquid and  membrane material
texts.text: Contact angle  for  liquid and  membrane material

texts.label: text
texts.orig: A  5 M  aqueous solution of monoethanolamine in  water  has  a surface  tension of  around 58  1  O-'   N/m  and  exhibits a contact angle  of 98  degrees  with  teflon. For  an  allowable excess  pressure of  1 bar  the  pore  radius  needs  to be  smaller than 0.2  pm.  However, porous teflon hollow  fibre  membranes with  pore  sizes  below  0.2  pm  are  not  commercially available yet.  Porous polyolefin hollow tibre membranes are  commercially available at  low  costs  with pore  sizes ranging from 0.01 to  0.2  pm. An alternative to  the  use  of  a  MEA-teflon combination is  a  combination of  an absorption liquid of  surface tension similar to  water and  polyolefin membranes. TN0 is  currently developing the latter combination.
texts.text: A  5 M  aqueous solution of monoethanolamine in  water  has  a surface  tension of  around 58  1  O-'   N/m  and  exhibits a contact angle  of 98  degrees  with  teflon. For  an  allowable excess  pressure of  1 bar  the  pore  radius  needs  to be  smaller than 0.2  pm.  However, porous teflon hollow  fibre  membranes with  pore  sizes  below  0.2  pm  are  not  commercially available yet.  Porous polyolefin hollow tibre membranes are  commercially available at  low  costs  with pore  sizes ranging from 0.01 to  0.2  pm. An alternative to  the  use  of  a  MEA-teflon combination is  a  combination of  an absorption liquid of  surface tension similar to  water and  polyolefin membranes. TN0 is  currently developing the latter combination.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The flue gas  containing carbon dioxide is  countercmrently brought into contact with  the  amine solution in the membrane absorber. The  rich  amine solution is  fed  via  a heat  exchanger to  the  regenerator where  carbon dioxide is  released at  high  temperature.
texts.text: The flue gas  containing carbon dioxide is  countercmrently brought into contact with  the  amine solution in the membrane absorber. The  rich  amine solution is  fed  via  a heat  exchanger to  the  regenerator where  carbon dioxide is  released at  high  temperature.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The  membrane absorber design cannot be  based on  conventional membrane module designs. These designs have historically been  based  on  the  use  of hollow  fibre  membranes for  filtration duties with  ill-defined flow  conditions on the  shell  side  of the  membrane. When  membranes are  used  for  contacting duties it  is necessary that  flow  conditions are  well-defined on  either  side  of the  membrane to  achieve  good  mass  transfer. Furthermore it  is essential that,  given the  large  volume flows  of flue  gas  streams, the  module design is  easy  to  scale  up.  TN0 has  patented a  membrane module design, shown  in  Figure 3,  which  has  good  mass  transfer characteristics and  good  scale-up potential.
texts.text: The  membrane absorber design cannot be  based on  conventional membrane module designs. These designs have historically been  based  on  the  use  of hollow  fibre  membranes for  filtration duties with  ill-defined flow  conditions on the  shell  side  of the  membrane. When  membranes are  used  for  contacting duties it  is necessary that  flow  conditions are  well-defined on  either  side  of the  membrane to  achieve  good  mass  transfer. Furthermore it  is essential that,  given the  large  volume flows  of flue  gas  streams, the  module design is  easy  to  scale  up.  TN0 has  patented a  membrane module design, shown  in  Figure 3,  which  has  good  mass  transfer characteristics and  good  scale-up potential.

texts.label: caption
texts.orig: Fig.  3.  TN0 cross-flow membrane module design
texts.text: Fig.  3.  TN0 cross-flow membrane module design

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The performance of  a  membrane absorber can be  compared with the  performance of  a  conventional packed column. The results are  shown  in  Figure 4 expressed as the  product of  a mass  transfer coefficient ks (m/s)  and the specific surface area  a  (mZ/m3)  for  a given  liquid flow  velocity.  The  performance has  been evaluated for  a situation in  which the  flue  gas  at  a  temperature of  4S°C  contains 3.5%  CO*.  80%  Of  the  carbon dioxide is  removed in  the absorber. A  mass  transfer analysis indicates that  the  mass  transfer resistance is  dominated by  the  resistance in  the liquid in  both cases.
texts.text: The performance of  a  membrane absorber can be  compared with the  performance of  a  conventional packed column. The results are  shown  in  Figure 4 expressed as the  product of  a mass  transfer coefficient ks (m/s)  and the specific surface area  a  (mZ/m3)  for  a given  liquid flow  velocity.  The  performance has  been evaluated for  a situation in  which the  flue  gas  at  a  temperature of  4S°C  contains 3.5%  CO*.  80%  Of  the  carbon dioxide is  removed in  the absorber. A  mass  transfer analysis indicates that  the  mass  transfer resistance is  dominated by  the  resistance in  the liquid in  both cases.

texts.label: caption
texts.orig: Fig.  4.  Performance comparison of  membrane absorber with  packed  column
texts.text: Fig.  4.  Performance comparison of  membrane absorber with  packed  column

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The  results of the  comparison show  that  a membrane absorber can  lead  to  a near  ten-fold reduction in  absorber size for  this  application.
texts.text: The  results of the  comparison show  that  a membrane absorber can  lead  to  a near  ten-fold reduction in  absorber size for  this  application.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 5.  REDUCTION OF  ENERGY CONSUMPTION OF  AIlSORPTION PROCESSES
texts.text: 5.  REDUCTION OF  ENERGY CONSUMPTION OF  AIlSORPTION PROCESSES

texts.label: text
texts.orig: An important drawback of  absorption processes for  carbon dioxide removal from flue  gases is  the large heat requirement of  the  desorption process. Table 1  gives  an indication of  the various contributions to  the energy consumption of  a monoethanolamine process (5M  MEA).
texts.text: An important drawback of  absorption processes for  carbon dioxide removal from flue  gases is  the large heat requirement of  the  desorption process. Table 1  gives  an indication of  the various contributions to  the energy consumption of  a monoethanolamine process (5M  MEA).

texts.label: page_header
texts.orig: FERON  and JANSEN: CAPTURE OF CO? USING  MEMBRANE GAS  ABSORPTION
texts.text: FERON  and JANSEN: CAPTURE OF CO? USING  MEMBRANE GAS  ABSORPTION

texts.label: caption
texts.orig: Table 1. Energy  consumption of  a monoethanolamine process
texts.text: Table 1. Energy  consumption of  a monoethanolamine process

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The  estimate for  the  total  heat  requirement compares well  with  the  results of plant modelling which  results  in  a heat requirement between 180 and  200  kJ/mole  CO,. The  energy  consumption is not  only  due  to  the  high  binding energy of  the  carbon dioxide with  monoethanolamine but also  due  to  the  evaporation of water. Per  mole  COs  between 1 and  2 moles  of water  need  to  be  evaporated which  makes  up  between 30%  and  40%  of the  energy  consumption. In the  future an  energy  consumption ten  times  the  minimum energy requirement should be  considered feasible. This means that  there is  scope  to  reduce the  energy  consumption significantly. This reduction can  be  achieved by  three types  of measures or  a combination of these:
texts.text: The  estimate for  the  total  heat  requirement compares well  with  the  results of plant modelling which  results  in  a heat requirement between 180 and  200  kJ/mole  CO,. The  energy  consumption is not  only  due  to  the  high  binding energy of  the  carbon dioxide with  monoethanolamine but also  due  to  the  evaporation of water. Per  mole  COs  between 1 and  2 moles  of water  need  to  be  evaporated which  makes  up  between 30%  and  40%  of the  energy  consumption. In the  future an  energy  consumption ten  times  the  minimum energy requirement should be  considered feasible. This means that  there is  scope  to  reduce the  energy  consumption significantly. This reduction can  be  achieved by  three types  of measures or  a combination of these:

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: -Use  of  sterically hindered amines or  mixtures of  amines with  lower  desorption energy requirement,
texts.text: -Use  of  sterically hindered amines or  mixtures of  amines with  lower  desorption energy requirement,

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: -Recovery of  latent heat  from  desorption process,
texts.text: -Recovery of  latent heat  from  desorption process,

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: -Application of  pervaporation for  regeneration which  limits the  evaporation of water.
texts.text: -Application of  pervaporation for  regeneration which  limits the  evaporation of water.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The  feasibility of the  membrane gas  absorption process  for  the  production of 25  tonnes per  hour  of carbon dioxide for the horticultural industry has been assessed. Preliminary designs of  the necessary equipment (membrane absorber, heat  exchangers and  desorber) have  been made  for  80%  removal of  carbon dioxide from  the  flue  gas of a gas-fired combined cycle  (3.4% CO,) based on  ASPEN Plus process calculations. Investment costs  have  been estimated for  the  various components. The  absorption liquid is  assumed to  be  regenerated by  the  waste  heat  from the combined cycle. As  investment costs are dominant the costs of  the carbon dioxide produced has been determined as  a function of the  time  of operation. Figure 5 shows  the  carbon dioxide production costs  in  arbitrary units  compared with  the  alternatives of carbon dioxide  delivery  from  an  industrial gas supplier and  the  usual  practice of  natural gas  combustion.
texts.text: The  feasibility of the  membrane gas  absorption process  for  the  production of 25  tonnes per  hour  of carbon dioxide for the horticultural industry has been assessed. Preliminary designs of  the necessary equipment (membrane absorber, heat  exchangers and  desorber) have  been made  for  80%  removal of  carbon dioxide from  the  flue  gas of a gas-fired combined cycle  (3.4% CO,) based on  ASPEN Plus process calculations. Investment costs  have  been estimated for  the  various components. The  absorption liquid is  assumed to  be  regenerated by  the  waste  heat  from the combined cycle. As  investment costs are dominant the costs of  the carbon dioxide produced has been determined as  a function of the  time  of operation. Figure 5 shows  the  carbon dioxide production costs  in  arbitrary units  compared with  the  alternatives of carbon dioxide  delivery  from  an  industrial gas supplier and  the  usual  practice of  natural gas  combustion.

texts.label: caption
texts.orig: Fig.  5:  Carbon dioxide production costs  as  a function of time of  operation
texts.text: Fig.  5:  Carbon dioxide production costs  as  a function of time of  operation

texts.orig: GAS-FIRED COMBINED CYCLE Parameter : Annuity tactor
texts.text: GAS-FIRED COMBINED CYCLE Parameter : Annuity tactor

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Figure 5  shows that carbon dioxide production with a  membrane gas  absorption process is  cheaper than the alternatives. Only at  low  operation time  the  alternatives are  more cost-effective.
texts.text: Figure 5  shows that carbon dioxide production with a  membrane gas  absorption process is  cheaper than the alternatives. Only at  low  operation time  the  alternatives are  more cost-effective.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Membrane gas  absorption is  an  efficient process to  remove carbon dioxide from  flue  gases.  Membrane absorbers have size  advantages over  conventional packed columns. A  stable operation of  the  membrane absorber can be achieved by  the  use  suitable combinations of  absorption liquids and  membranes which  still  requires development work. The energy consumption of  the absorption proces can be decreased by alternative absorption liquids, recovery of  latent heat  and  regeneration by  pervaporation. A feasibility study of  carbon dioxide producution using membrane gas absorption for horticultural applications indicates that the process is  cheaper than alternative methods.
texts.text: Membrane gas  absorption is  an  efficient process to  remove carbon dioxide from  flue  gases.  Membrane absorbers have size  advantages over  conventional packed columns. A  stable operation of  the  membrane absorber can be achieved by  the  use  suitable combinations of  absorption liquids and  membranes which  still  requires development work. The energy consumption of  the absorption proces can be decreased by alternative absorption liquids, recovery of  latent heat  and  regeneration by  pervaporation. A feasibility study of  carbon dioxide producution using membrane gas absorption for horticultural applications indicates that the process is  cheaper than alternative methods.
